results.title: Missing authorization in cancel() allows anyone to cancel any order
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports Issue #2: cancel() Missing Authorization Allows Anyone to Cancel Any Order, stating cancel() only checks existence and does not verify msg.sender == order.maker. It references the same function/code path and proposes the same fix (add an authorization check reverting Unauthorized). Fixing as recommended in the audit would fix the vulnerability described.

results.title: Linked list corruption enables double refund via incomplete order cleanup
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item 5 explicitly flags that _cancelOrder does not clear remaining, prev, and next, leaving stale pointers and values. This is the same root cause and code path as the described vulnerability (incomplete order cleanup in _cancelOrder leading to linked list corruption). The specific fix (clear order.prev, order.next, and order.remaining after unlinking) would address both. Although the audit downplays exploitability and focuses on data consistency, it still identifies the exact missing deletions in the same function/fields, so the vulnerability was detected.

results.title: Integer underflow in balance subtraction allows draining DEX liquidity
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #1 describes the exact same bug: unchecked subtraction in _processWithdrawal called by emergencyWithdraw without a prior balance check, causing uint128 underflow and enabling any user to drain the contract. It cites the same code path (emergencyWithdraw -> _processWithdrawal, balances[user][token] -= amount in unchecked) and impact (complete fund drain). The same fix (add sufficient-balance check and avoid unchecked arithmetic) would resolve both.
